query: diagnosis and management of leaky meningomyelocele in infants

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Dysraphism and Myelomeningocele(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The initial intervention for a patient with myelomeningocele includes intravenous antibiotics, neurosurgical closure of the defect within 24 to 48 hours of birth to prevent infections and later complications, and ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement if the patient develops hydrocephalus. If the infant is in a critical status secondary to respiratory failure, sepsis, or other gross congenital organ anomalies causing life-threatening problems, initial supportive care is recommended. [28] The surgical procedure involves reversing the failed steps of normal spinal cord closure employing extensive knowledge of normal anatomy and embryology. [16] It is carried out under general anesthesia with an endotracheal tube. In the first step, the neural placode is separated, tubularized, and then stitched. After that, the dura is separated from the underlying tissues and closed. Then the skin is approximated and sutured. [1] Postoperative care has a vital role in the success of the surgery. The patient should be given prophylactic antibiotics, painkillers, and good nutrition. It is important to ensure that patient is nursed in a prone position to avoid aspiration and wound dehiscence. Wound care is essential to avoid wound infection. Dressing change and avoiding urinary and fecal contamination of the wound is necessary. The patient should also be monitored and treated for urinary problems. [8] Recently, specialized centers are performing fetal myelomeningocele closure. This reduces the development of hydrocephalus, but at the risk of an increase in prenatal complications and preterm birth. [16] Meningocele repair should be performed in an elective setting as the skin is usually intact. Surgical repair is followed by a lifetime follow-up by a multidisciplinary team. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningocele -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate meningocele from similar spinal anomalies through imaging techniques, particularly using magnetic resonance imaging, to avoid misdiagnosis. Screen at-risk pregnancies using maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein and ultrasound for early detection of meningocele and associated neural tube defects. Implement recommended prenatal and postnatal imaging protocols to assess meningocele and detect any associated conditions like tethered cord syndrome. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes for patients with a meningocele. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Routine surveillance in an orthopedic clinic also helps promote ambulation and manage deformities like scoliosis and contractures. Worsening scoliosis could be a sign of complications like tethered cord, shunt malfunction, hydromyelia, among others, and must trigger immediate evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Early surgical intervention within 24 to 48 hours after birth is crucial to reducing the risk of infection and enhancing quality of life. This approach ensures access to therapeutic services tailored to the patient's needs when combined with interprofessional care. Early surgical management has demonstrated effectiveness in improving survival rates and minimizing complications. Children with myelomeningocele often require regular medical evaluations and hospital visits to address their ongoing needs and concerns. Early medical intervention is significant for those with learning and cognitive disabilities, as it can help equip them to overcome future educational challenges.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Recent medical advances have greatly enhanced the quality of life for children with myelomeningocele. Early identification and management of associated health issues are vital to preventing complications, particularly those impacting mobility, as well as bowel and bladder function. Prompt interprofessional medical and surgical care is critical to minimizing deficits and maximizing the potential for future improvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: A child’s diagnosis will affect their quality of life. The best way to support the child will be to take them to a multidisciplinary team of medical professionals who specialize in taking care of patients with this diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: After receiving a prenatal diagnosis of spina bifida, some parents chose to terminate the pregnancy, while others prefer to continue their pregnancy. If you decide to continue the pregnancy, some centers can offer fetal surgery to correct the defect, or scheduled surgery to correct the congenital disability immediately after birth. After the initial treatment, your baby will need medical management throughout their life. They may need: Wheelchair or assist devices to help them walk Other surgeries to fix their bones They may have learning problems and require additional testing and support at school If their bladder does not work, they may need a tube to empty their bladder from time to time.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Spina bifida is a congenital disability in which the backbone or spine does not typically form in a baby while developing in the mother’s womb. It is also known as myelomeningocele or open neural tube defect. It will appear an opening in their skin on their back through which part of the spine and nerve tissue can protrude out. Neural tube defects can cause various long-term problems in your child, and they depend on how severe the defect is. Many babies with spina bifida can have the following: A weakness of their leg and may not be able to walk Loss of feeling below their waist Problems controlling their urine and stools Issues of learning or memory Some children may also develop too much fluid in their brain and may need surgery to relieve the pressure on their brain

retrieved_docs.title: Meningocele -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Meningocele usually has no neural involvement or acute neurological complications, hence a favorable prognosis. Spina bifida aperta usually presents a skin defect with the looming risk of cerebral spinal fluid leak, whereas the occult forms have no visible skin opening. The prevalence of spina bifida at birth has decreased by 23% over 10 years between 1995 and 2004 after the Food and Drug Administration mandated fortification of all cereal and grain products with folic acid. In clinical practice, the prognosis of patients with meningocele is excellent, and simple surgical repair is adequate. [3] [6] [4] [6] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningocele -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Meningocele is a type of neural tube defect where the meninges, or protective membranes around the spinal cord, protrude through a spinal defect, forming a sac filled with cerebrospinal fluid. Unlike more severe forms of spina bifida, such as myelomeningocele, meningocele typically does not involve the spinal cord or nerves and, therefore, generally presents without neurological symptoms. Diagnosis is primarily achieved through magnetic resonance imaging and, in prenatal cases, may be identified through maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein screening and ultrasound. Prognosis is excellent following surgical repair, although ongoing monitoring is necessary to detect associated conditions like tethered cord syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal diagnosis is typically achieved through maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein levels and ultrasonography, which reveal characteristic cranial and spinal signs. Management includes early postnatal surgical closure or prenatal repair to reduce complications such as hindbrain herniation. Treatment may also address associated hydrocephalus and Chiari II malformations through shunt placement or endoscopic surgery, aimed at preventing further neurological decline and supporting optimal development. This activity provides healthcare professionals with the knowledge and skills to recognize myelomeningoceles, conduct the recommended evaluations, and implement an interprofessional management approach to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal surgery has increasingly become common in the treatment of myelomeningocele. Animal studies have shown that the myelomeningocele lesion's intrauterine closure will prevent the neural tissue's progressive intrauterine damage due to exposure to amniotic fluid. [15] A randomized control trial comparing prenatal surgery's efficacy vs. postnatal repair was done and was stopped early due to the evident better outcomes with prenatal surgery. The trial showed that prenatal surgery was associated with a decreased need for shunt placement (40% in the prenatal group and 82% in the postnatal surgery group). It also showed that children who underwent prenatal surgery had improved mental development and motor function at 30 months. Complications associated with prenatal surgery were increased risk of prenatal delivery and uterine dehiscence at the time of delivery. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with myelomeningocele frequently experience fluid accumulation in the brain, such as hydrocephalus, which leads to excessive pressure within the skull. For children diagnosed with hydrocephalus, shunt surgery may be required to insert a hollow tube into the brain, allowing excess fluid to drain from the ventricles. Additionally, clinicians should counsel patients to avoid latex products, as children with myelomeningocele are at risk for latex allergies.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningocele -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Strong communication among team members ensures cohesive care planning. Moreover, dedicated spina bifida clinics can facilitate increased streamlined coordination. Effective communication and coordination strategies allow for comprehensive evaluation and treatment strategies that improve patient outcomes and overall team performance.

retrieved_docs.title: Neural Tube Disorders(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Postnatal Assessment and Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the absence of prenatal screening, children with NTDs require immediate assessment after birth about the site of any defects, size, and leakage of cerebrospinal fluid, and any infections, which should be managed accordingly. Ensure aseptic measures during the assessment. The use of non-latex gloves reduces the risk of latex sensitization. Broad-spectrum antibiotics coverage to prevent infections, sterile and saline-soaked dressing, the neurological assessment helps in a wide range to facilitate postnatal life. The surgical management of closed neural tube defects is challenging and complex. Practitioners must be familiar with the underlying embryology associated with the specific defect and the contemporary surgical management to reduce the risk of CSF fistula formation or neurologic compromise, which leads to high morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningomyelocele(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: When the infant is born, careful assessment of the lesion should be done while laying the infant in the lateral or prone position. Non-latex gloves should be used to prevent latex sensitization [17] . The lesion should be covered by a moist dressing or plastic wrap to prevent heat loss. In postnatal surgery, closure of myelomeningocele should be done as early as possible to reduce infection risk (ideally within the first 48 hours after birth). [18] In patients with associated Chiari II malformation, presenting with brainstem dysfunction, decompressive upper cervical laminectomy may be needed to reduce brainstem and cerebellar tonsillar decompression. [19] Most of these patients benefit from a multidisciplinary team approach for coordinating their management. In patients with urinary dysfunction, daily catheterization decreases urine stasis and therefore reduce urinary tract infections. An orthopedic follow-up should be arranged to evaluate and manage associated complications, like contractures and scoliosis. [20] Delivery should preferably be done at a center with a high-care-level neonatal intensive care unit where pediatric neurosurgery services are available. Delivery should be performed at term to prevent complications related to prematurity. When in utero surgery is performed, a cesarean section is recommended at 37 weeks of gestation or earlier ( if maternal indication) to decrease the uterine rupture's associated risk. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 834
retrieved_docs.content: event that can affect fetal well‑being and labor progress. Overall, the network of relationships shows that MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY integrates fetal developmental disorders (such as myelomeningocele, hydrocephalus, and fluid accumulations), maternal pelvic dynamics (pelvic inlet, outlet, and overall contraction), and standard delivery practices (normal delivery procedure and spontaneous vaginal delivery). Recognizing how these entities interrelate is essential for clinicians to assess risk, plan appropriate interventions, and optimize outcomes for both mother and fetus during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is linked to other prenatal abnormalities, including wanverhouding; foetale ascites and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion). Recognizing that fetal disproportion associated with myelomeningocele, as well as ascites and hydrocephalus, all occur within the same developmental window underscores the importance of comprehensive fetal assessment to identify and manage multiple concurrent anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2149
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a prenatal growth abnormality that manifests during the fetal period. In the same developmental window, other fluid‑related fetal conditions also arise, including wanverhouding with foetale ascites, wanverhouding with foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the fetal period, reflecting a spectrum of fetal fluid accumulation and structural imbalance that can be identified through prenatal imaging and require coordinated obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: term malposition/malpresentation within obstetric terminology. These findings reflect عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (instability of fetal position) and are used in clinical guidelines for “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” to guide recognition, classification, and management, which may include specialized labor management, operative delivery, cesarean section, or intensified monitoring to protect maternal and neonatal safety. Cephalopelvic disproportion (عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض) is a child concept of Fetal Positions and Presentations and is further connected to structural fetal anomalies such as fetal myelomeningocele and fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that cause the mismatch. The fetal period is also associated with pathological states including foetale ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), foetale hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops), and other fetal period pathologies that can contribute to abnormal presentations. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 834
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that encompasses a variety of specific fetal and obstetric entities, procedural concepts, and anatomical factors relevant to the process of childbirth. Within this category, the condition Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation is listed, indicating a successful repositioning of a breech fetus to a head‑down orientation prior to delivery. The category also includes Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, a neural‑tube defect that can cause size mismatch between the fetus and the maternal pelvis, and this specific condition is situated within the Fetal period, highlighting its occurrence during fetal development. Several fetal abnormalities that affect labor are enumerated as child concepts of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY. These include سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition), استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 946
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that encompasses a variety of obstetric and fetal abnormalities known to influence the course of pregnancy, the fetal period, and the process of labor and delivery. Maternal cervical factors listed under this category include a cervical cerclage suture present, a cicatrix of the cervix affecting pregnancy, a polyp of the cervix affecting pregnancy, and a previous operation to the cervix affecting pregnancy; each of these structural cervical changes can compromise cervical competence, increase the risk of preterm labor, or create mechanical obstacles to vaginal delivery. Fetal conditions captured within the same category comprise fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that also leads to disproportion, fetal ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), and fetal hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops); all of these abnormalities arise during the fetal period and can result in abnormal fetal size, fluid accumulation, or impaired fetal physiology, thereby complicating the mechanics of labor. Additionally, multiple gestations such as twin pregnancy (حمل توأمي), triplet pregnancy (حمل بثلاثة توائم), and quadruplet pregnancy (حمل بأربعة توائم) are included, reflecting the increased obstetric challenges associated with higher-order multiples, including uterine overdistension and heightened risk of malpresentation. The category also records a breech malpresentation successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, illustrating that corrective maneuvers can mitigate one potential labor difficulty. Collectively, these maternal cervical abnormalities, fetal structural and fluid‑related disorders, and multiple gestations represent key factors that clinicians must assess and manage throughout the fetal period to optimize labor outcomes and ensure safe delivery for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 870
retrieved_docs.content: are all noted to occur in the fetal period. The interrelationship among these entities underscores that a range of maternal conditions (such as cervical polyps) and fetal conditions (including myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, ascites, hydrops fetalis, and hydrocephalus) can directly affect labor and delivery outcomes. Recognizing that these specific complications belong to the overarching category of maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery, and that the fetal abnormalities develop during the fetal period, is essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version for breech or surgical management of cervical polyps), and optimize both maternal and neonatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 730
retrieved_docs.content: The collection of maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery encompasses a broad spectrum of obstetric findings, interventions, and fetal abnormalities that together shape the course of pregnancy, labor, and birth. Successful conversion of a breech malpresentation to a cephalic presentation, the presence of a cervical cerclage suture, and fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele are specific conditions classified within this group. Pelvic contractile abnormalities—تقلص الحوض (overall pelvic contraction), تقلص مدخل الحوض (pelvic inlet contraction), and تقلص مخرج الحوض (pelvic outlet contraction)—are also included, as they can alter the mechanics of labor. Normal delivery procedures and normal pregnancy & or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery) are related concepts that describe the expected pathway when these conditions are absent or adequately managed. Fetal anomalies that may arise during the fetal period, such as wanverhouding; foetale ascites (fetal ascites), wanverhouding; foetale hydrops (fetal hydrops), استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion), and سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition), are likewise considered part of the maternal‑and‑fetal condition spectrum. The fetal period itself is the developmental stage in which these fetal abnormalities occur, and they can directly contribute to labor difficulties such as disproportion or malpresentation. Recognizing and addressing each of these maternal and fetal factors—through presentation conversion, cervical cerclage, monitoring of pelvic contractility, and management of fetal ascites, hydrops, hydrocephalus, and malposition—helps clinicians optimize labor management and improve outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
retrieved_docs.content: Funic presentation, also referred to as cord presentation, is classified as a type of سوء المجيء الجنيني (abnormal fetal presentation). It occurs when the umbilical cord descends into the birth canal ahead of the fetus, increasing the risk of cord compression, fetal distress, and delivery complications. Recognizing funic presentation as a subset of سوء المجيء الجنيني is important for obstetric management, prompting careful monitoring and, when necessary, interventions such as cesarean delivery to protect maternal and neonatal health.
